text: Data parallelism trains replicas of a model on different mini-{{c1::batches}} and synchronizes gradients across workers.
explanation: It is the standard way to scale training across multiple GPUs or machines.
usage: Key in large-scale deep learning and distributed training pipelines.
spanish: El paralelismo de datos entrena réplicas del modelo en mini-batches diferentes y sincroniza gradientes entre workers.

text: Model parallelism splits layers or parameters across devices when a single model is too large for one {{c1::GPU}}.
explanation: It is essential for very large transformer and diffusion models.
usage: Used for trillion-parameter or frontier-scale training runs.
spanish: El paralelismo de modelo divide capas o parámetros entre dispositivos cuando un modelo es demasiado grande para una GPU.

text: Gradient accumulation simulates a larger {{c1::batch}} by accumulating gradients across several smaller steps.
explanation: It reduces memory pressure while preserving the effective batch size.
usage: Common in large language model fine-tuning and low-memory settings.
spanish: La acumulación de gradientes simula un batch mayor acumulando gradientes a través de varios pasos pequeños.

text: Mixed precision training uses lower-precision arithmetic to improve {{c1::throughput}} and reduce memory cost.
explanation: It is now standard in modern GPU training stacks.
usage: Widely used in transformer pretraining and large-scale inference preparation.
spanish: El entrenamiento con precisión mixta usa aritmética de menor precisión para mejorar throughput y reducir costo de memoria.

text: Gradient checkpointing trades extra computation for reduced memory during {{c1::backpropagation}}.
explanation: It helps fit larger models when memory is the binding constraint.
usage: Important for deep networks and long-context LLM training.
spanish: El checkpointing de gradientes intercambia cómputo extra por menos memoria durante backpropagation.

text: {{c1::Learning rate}} schedules adapt the step size over time to improve convergence and stability.
explanation: Proper scheduling can be as important as the optimizer itself.
usage: Fundamental in optimization for deep learning and RL.
spanish: Los schedules de learning rate adaptan el tamaño del paso con el tiempo para mejorar convergencia y estabilidad.

scenario: Math & Physics 📐 (Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) Loss (Rafailov et al., 2023))
text: Ecuación para {{c1::Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) Loss (Rafailov et al., 2023)}}: \[\mathcal{L}_{\text{DPO}}(\pi_\theta; \pi_{\text{ref}}) = -\mathbb{E}_{(x, y_w, y_l)}\left[\log \sigma\left(\beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_w|x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_w|x)} - \beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_l|x)}{\pi_{\text{ref}}(y_l|x)}\right)\right]\]
explanation:
Desglose de variables:
  • \(\pi_\theta\): Policy model parameter weights optimized.
  • \(\pi_{\text{ref}}\): Frozen reference model weights (typically SFT model).
  • \(y_w, y_l\): Preferred (winner) and dispreferred (loser) outputs for prompt \(x\).
  • \(\beta\): Divergence regularization parameter.
  • \(\sigma\): Sigmoid activation function.
spanish: Fórmula de Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) Loss (Rafailov et al., 2023)

text: ¿Cómo funciona la estructura de {{c1::RLHF vs. DPO Pipelines}} en el sistema?
explanation:
DPO shows mathematically that the reward function can be expressed in terms of the optimal policy. It bypasses training a separate reward model and utilizing complex reinforcement learning (PPO), updating the policy directly via relative log-likelihoods on prompt preference pairs.

graph TD subgraph RLHF ["Traditional RLHF Pipeline"] SFT1["SFT Model"] --> RM["Train Reward Model (RM)"] RM --> PPO["Train Policy with PPO (Actor-Critic loops)"] end subgraph DPO ["Direct Preference Optimization"] SFT2["SFT Model"] --> DPOLoss["Direct Policy Updates (DPO loss over preference pairs)"] end
usage:
Visual model for RLHF vs. DPO Pipelines.
Graph:
graph TD subgraph RLHF ["Traditional RLHF Pipeline"] SFT1["SFT Model"] --> RM["Train Reward Model (RM)"] RM --> PPO["Train Policy with PPO (Actor-Critic loops)"] end subgraph DPO ["Direct Preference Optimization"] SFT2["SFT Model"] --> DPOLoss["Direct Policy Updates (DPO loss over preference pairs)"] end
spanish: DPO demuestra matemáticamente que la función de recompensa se puede expresar a partir de la política óptima. Esto permite saltarse el entrenamiento de un modelo de recompensa separado y el uso de PPO.

text:
Pregunta: How does the XLA (Accelerated Linear Algebra) compiler improve TPU execution speeds compared to eager execution?
Opciones:
  • A) By JIT compiling high-level operators into fused kernels, reducing memory bandwidth overhead
  • B) By running calculations in INT4 quantization format natively on Tensor Cores
  • C) By dynamically caching GPU activation weights during backward passes
  • D) By allocating memory pages directly to CPU host threads via unified drivers

Respuesta correcta: {{c1::A) By JIT compiling high-level operators into fused kernels, reducing memory bandwidth overhead}}
explanation: Justificación: XLA fuses multiple operations together into single hardware kernels. Instead of executing addition, multiplication, and reductions sequentially (requiring writing intermediate tensors back to slow memory), XLA compiles them into a single kernel that performs all operations in registers, saving memory bandwidth.
usage: Quiz assertion: A) By JIT compiling high-level operators into fused kernels, reducing memory bandwidth overhead
spanish: Quiz: How does the XLA (Accelerated Linear Algebra) compiler improve TPU execution speeds compared to eager execution?

text:
Patrón de código en python para {{c1::JAX SPMD Parallel Partitioning (pmap)}}:
import jax
import jax.numpy as jnp

# Define a simple function
def add_constant(x):
    return x + 10.0

# Compile for parallel execution across all TPU/GPU devices
parallel_add = jax.pmap(add_constant)

# Prepare input array (size must equal local device count)
devices = jax.local_device_count()
x = jnp.arange(devices) * 1.5
y = parallel_add(x)
print(f"Devices: {devices}, Input: {x}, Output: {y}")
explanation: Demonstrates JAX's Single Program Multiple Data (SPMD) paradigm where `jax.pmap` compiles a function and replicates execution across multiple TPU cores in parallel.
usage:
Snippet:

# Define a simple function

    return x + 10.0

# Compile for parallel execution across all TPU/GPU devices

# Prepare input array (size must equal local device count)

x = jnp.arange(devices) * 1.5
y = parallel_add(x)

Output expected: Success
spanish: Patrón de código: JAX SPMD Parallel Partitioning (pmap)
